**Part II: Learning to See**

# The Speaking of Names

*Initiation*

---

When you study this thoroughly — as I have, as others before me have — you start to see cracks in the world. Things you don't yet have names for. You start to find others who have tried to name what we are seeing, to cast spells that help us read this unmapped terrain. Sometimes — as Paulo Freire did — just incanting the name is powerful enough to change things.

This chapter is an *incantation* — naming some of the demons you will encounter on your journey to unmask them, and at the end, summoning a powerful aid and guide to support you.

Let us proceed.

These forces are intelligent. Not conspiratorial — they have no plan. They operate according to their nature, and their nature has been operating on you since before you could name it. A spellbook is a bestiary that tells you what to do with what you have named. Once a thing has a seal, you can see it coming. You can say *that is what this is* and the room changes.

Not all of them were bred on purpose. Most have no author. They run on their nature and persist because they pay — and would go on paying if every hand that raised them forgot why. But some were drawn deliberately: the color line was written into law, enforced, intended, a demon whose makers knew its name. The bestiary holds both, and the seal tells them apart.

It tells you one thing more — the thing to know before you name anything. Not every hardship is a demon. Some weight is only weight. A demon is the harm that has someone to serve and a hand to keep it so. Where a hurt has neither — no one it profits, no one defending it — it is grief, and grief is carried, not fought. So name which you face. Mistake the one for the other, and you will fight the weather, or bow to a thing that could have been bound.

Every tradition has its version of this. Freire called it *conscientização* — the process by which people name the structures that have been making them silent, and discover that the naming breaks the silence. "The word is not the privilege of some few men," he wrote, "but the right of every man." To say the name of what is crushing you is the first political act. Everything else follows.

The circle is already drawn. You are holding the spellbook. The naming begins now.

---

*Production of subjectivity.* Hardt and Negri's name. I first encountered it in *Commonwealth*, in May 2020, two months after the world had rearranged itself into something unrecognizable. The phrase grabbed hold of my mind and wouldn't let go. I had the answer before I had the question. It would take five more years — and a restaurant — to understand what I had been handed.

There are coffee shops that double as salons — if the right people are there on the right night, theory meets whatever is actually happening and stays for hours. I've been having these conversations for years. One night we were working through a housing policy dispute: which factions held what interests, whose assumptions about political legitimacy matched their actual position, who carried responsibility for outcomes and who got credit without it. I wasn't talking about production of subjectivity. I was watching it happen.

It was the transition from seeing the track to seeing the foot that made it. In this case, I could see the jackboot.

I let it run. Between the words, I could hear the shapes of who carried responsibility and who held authority — and how those assignments weren't coming from the argument. They were already there. Produced before anyone walked in. Something outside the room had written the script.

I saw it being made.

I tried to interrupt its production. Introduced ideas incompatible with the frame, handed my friends a different lens. They shrugged. I had what seemed like a master key, and it didn't open anything. I thought I was a bad communicator. What I was, was impatient. This task takes generations. That is a significant part of why I'm writing this book.

Power does not only produce goods. It produces people. Any power — state, capital, church, institution — that structures how you live is simultaneously producing the kind of person legible to it. Employee, subject, consumer, believer. You know in the body, before the thought, what is being asked and how to deliver it. This production is not incidental. It is the primary output.

Capital produces the bodies that capital needs.

The school that produces obedient test-takers is not secretly coordinating with the factory that needs obedient line workers. They evolved in the same ecosystem, under the same pressures, toward the same adaptive solution. The result is a population shaped — in their habits, their reflexes, their instinct for what not to notice — for the system that shaped them. The override happens before it registers as an override. Practiced long enough, it stops registering at all. The body goes quiet. The amnesia is the product.

When you try to build something that requires a different kind of person, you discover this quickly.

---

There was a young man at the restaurant. I will not describe him closely enough to identify him. He was intelligent — genuinely, visibly, the kind of intelligence that makes you want to teach. He was motivated. He was an artist, self-taught, with a real gift. I thought: here is someone who will respond to what I actually know how to do.

My methods are built on autonomy. Not the sink-or-swim version — I scaffold the necessary knowledge, create the motivation, verify the skills, then create the conditions in which those skills are needed. After that point, the individual's instincts should take over. Not because they were told to. Because the problem is in front of them and they have what it takes to meet it.

His instincts didn't take over.

I pulled out everything I knew. Invented new approaches. Designed training structures I hadn't used before. Convinced myself I was solving an individual problem with an individual solution. I kept adjusting the scaffold, convinced the scaffold was the issue.

I was attacking the wrong problem.

What I eventually saw — after he was gone, after the exhaustion lifted enough to let me look clearly — was that he relied entirely on an authoritarian mode of perception. Without explicit permission, instruction, and external discipline, he could not act on something he cared about. Not because he didn't care. Not because he lacked capability. Because the system that had produced him had produced him for a specific kind of world: one in which someone else always holds the frame.

In that world, my offer of autonomy was not liberation. It was abandonment. The freedom I was extending was not recognizable as freedom because it had no authority behind it. Nobody had sanctioned it. Nobody had told him this was the correct thing to want. And without that sanction, the wanting couldn't quite activate.

This is production of subjectivity at close range. The system had not failed to educate him. It had educated him completely — for itself. The creativity was there. The motivation was there. But they were tethered to a permission structure that I could not replicate and would not manufacture, because the permission structure is the problem.

*I was trying to offer a commons to someone who had been produced for an enclosure.* The connection was missing before the scaffold went up. I didn't see it until I was deep in the attempt.

He was waiting for the clipboard. I had refused to lift it. The spell he had been raised inside has names.

---

*Biopower.* Foucault's name. The apparatus. The clipboard and the whip.

You know it by what happens in the body before the words arrive: the breath catching in the throat, the diaphragm stopping, the freeze. The swallowed word. The rage that finds no room.

We have sometimes called this kind of power parasitic. That is not quite right. A parasite eats its host until it dies, which kills the parasite — which is why true parasitism is rare. What we are looking at is the smiling parasite: the one that keeps the host healthy enough to keep giving, and calls this care.

Biopower manages life. Not the threat of death — that is older and cruder. This is the demon that discovered it was more productive to administer the conditions of living. To count, to school, to medicate, to zone, to credentialize, to license.

The domains: school makes thought legible. Work makes activity legible. Real estate makes environment legible. Cities make society legible. Media makes culture legible. Prisons make the body legible. Each domain has its own clipboard. They all serve the same demon.

Biopower appears as whatever you might desire. Its price is always the same: your soul.

Not the forbidden fruit — the opposite. This is the fruit you think you need to live, that you will not realize you don't need until much later, if ever. It holds your own life out to you as if it's making you a deal. As if it's offering you a place to belong.

The deal is consistent across every domain. Allow me to control your life and I will give you a share of the controlling. Hold the whip sometimes. Mark the test. Sign the permit. Issue the parole. The hierarchy of biopower is not who escapes it. It is who is allowed to wield it on others. This is the function, form, process, and purpose of social status.

The best metaphor I have is a car dealership. It remakes the total environment in its own image to justify its own existence. The infrastructure for alternatives disappears. The need for what it sells grows. It does not force this. It administers it.

Subjectivity is what spills out the side of all of this.

A tree produces everything it needs, everything everyone around it needs, and then has a surplus for generations it will never meet. Someone eats the flowers. Someone eats the fruit. The ones who eat the fruit carry the seeds — not all of them, just enough. Mycelium runs between the roots, exchanging nutrients, amplifying what each tree can reach on its own. The tree is habitat, thermal stabilization, hydrologic regulation, alchemist working on solar time. *Cradle to Cradle* called this abundance, not waste.

Biopower manages the orchard. It wants the fruit. But it cannot have the fruit without the tree, and the tree cannot be reduced to its fruit without ceasing to be a tree. The flowers, the mycelium, the seeds, the shade, the changed rain — these are not byproducts. They are the living system. The fruit is just one part of it.

The same is true of the subjects biopower produces. It needs legible ones. What it gets are subjects producing each other, in side conversations, in the spaces between the clipboard's boxes. The whisperer was always inside the orchard. The orchard cannot stop what the tree is. That overflow is also where you live.

*Biopolitics* is the name for the contest. Not the apparatus, not the effect — the fight between them.

Deleuze and Guattari describe something like this in *A Thousand Plateaus*: the inscription of strata, layer on layer, each layer a record of the pressures that produced it. The refrain — a bird's territorial song — as inscription, as the daily renegotiation of where things stand. Not evolution. Evolutionary.

Think of a forest. Every creature in it is attending to and adjusting to the patterns of every other creature. Coyotes noting where the cougars have been. Wolves reading the wind for elk. Birds renegotiating territories through song, every morning, without pause. Ants laying pheromone trails that aggregate into roads. Bears pressing the same routes for generations until the trail becomes the landscape.

No one designed the trail system. The trail system is biopolitics: the accumulated inscription of every creature tracking and being tracked, moving and being moved. It is how the forest knows itself.

Gramsci writing in the prison cell was doing biopolitics. Freire teaching adults to read the word as the reading of the world was doing biopolitics. Picking up this book is doing biopolitics.

The wizard's name for this: *enchantment*. The slow incantation by which one view of the world becomes the background everyone breathes. Biopolitics is the field; the spell is what travels through it.

---

*Hegemony.* Gramsci's name. The script. The demon that writes the other demons' lines and calls it the natural order.

Hegemony is not domination by force. Force is what power uses when hegemony fails. Hegemony is the production of consent — the arrangement of ideas, institutions, and cultural forms such that the dominant order seems natural, obvious, inevitable. The subordinated person is not forced to accept the frame. They are produced inside it, shaped by it from infancy, such that accepting the frame feels like seeing reality clearly.

The specific sensation is a kind of ease — the frame moving with you, the words arriving before you choose them. You do not freeze in hegemony. You flow. The haze where the objection would be. The thought that does not quite form. The amnesia for what you were about to say.

Frantz Fanon discovered this inscribed into a colonial officer's nervous system — to that officer's surprise and dismay.

Hegemony is the demon that makes you think you aren't possessed.

The name is the beginning of the counter. Not the counter itself — the beginning. You cannot fight what you cannot see. And you cannot see what has been arranged to look like the natural order of things.

---

*The outside.* Luxemburg's name. Not a place on the map — a relation of power. The bouncer at the door and the kidnapper in the alley: it pushes certain people, and certain forms of existence, to the margin, and moves to separate them when they find each other.

The outside is made, not found — the enclosable-not-yet-enclosed, the remainder hegemony has marked as available and not yet taken. A colonizer looks at a colony and sees layers of outsides, each awaiting its turn. The machine needs this: terrain it can draw value from without full payment. This is not the excess of the system. It is the mechanism. We will follow Luxemburg's argument fully in the next chapter.

The outside is not always geographical. When the geographical frontier closes, the machine marks new ones. Debt. Attention. The privatization of seed and code and genetic information. The commons of care — the cooking, the nursing, the raising of children, the tending of the sick — which was never paid for and therefore never appeared in any economic analysis, and which Silvia Federici spent her career making visible.

The outside is wherever the accounting stops. And the commons, in every form, is perpetually at risk of becoming the next outside.

This is why the commons was not enclosed because it failed. It was enclosed because it worked. The commons that works is the commons that is worth enclosing.

*The Other.* Not the outside — its opposite.

The outside is a relation of power: terrain under hegemony, the margin made by excluding. Withdraw the power and the outside dissolves. The Other was never made by power at all. The Other is simply another singularity — a person, a creature, a forest, not-you — and it holds the possibility of *magic*: the meeting that becomes, briefly, a single thing that was not there before. Not everything beyond the accounting is the outside. Some things cannot be enclosed at all. The Other is the first of them, and it exists whether power acts on it or not.

---

*The corruption of the commons.* Hardt and Negri's name. The demon that never attacks head-on: it waits for the commons to flourish, then encloses the flowering. It does not need to beat what you build. It needs only to outlast the day you stop defending it.

You have already watched it work. The web was given away — no owner, no fee, the rules let into the public domain; a proprietary web would not have been the web at all. Then the gate moved. What had been the commons became the platform, and the platform set the price. The thing was never destroyed. It was captured while everyone still called it free.

Then the demon fed. Everything freely made — the code, the encyclopedia, the writing, the talk between strangers — was gathered into engines no outsider could open. The commons went in. What came out had a gate. This is corruption in its pure form: not the theft of a thing, but the capture of everything freely given.

Name its nature and its weakness shows. The demon makes nothing. It cannot write the code, or the song, or the knowing between people; it can only wait at the gate and charge toll. It needs the commons to exist before it can close them — so it is always late, and always second. What it feeds on is the one thing it can never make.

---

So do not wall the commons in for safety; a wall is only the next gate, and the demon is patient. The counter is not protection. It is rupture: break the enclosure open again, and re-common what spills out before the gate can move. The spell that freed the web the first time was just this, written down — take it, use it, share it, never close it. The commons is not a place you reach. It is a rupture you keep making. Make it again.

---

Here is the practice.

Not a theory. A practice. The naming is useless until it touches the body.

Find something that has been making you silent. Not something large and structural — something immediate. A meeting you left without saying what you were thinking. A conversation you avoided. A decision that was made around you while you participated in good faith and said nothing.

Do not reach for the explanation of why you were silent. Reach for the feeling first. The specific contraction in the chest, or the shrug of the shoulders, or the particular silence that lands like a weight. Feel where it lives.

Now ask: what is the name for what produced this?

It may be hegemony — the common sense that made your objection seem impolite, disruptive, beside the point. It may be the production of subjectivity — the training that told you your role was to participate, not to shape. It may be biopower — the apparatus that made staying inside the frame feel like good sense, and stepping outside it feel like exposure. It may be the outside: care labor you were doing invisibly, attention extracted without acknowledgment, creativity consumed without compensation.

The name will not fix it. The name is the first move.

You will know you have found it when something lifts — when reality cracks a little and lets some light in. It might hurt. It might feel scary. Your challenge is to gather the courage to discern whether the name is true.

Once you have named the demon and traced its seal, you have something to work with. You can call it by its name. You can see it when it arrives. You can say to others: that is what this is.

This is what Freire's literacy circles did — not teach reading as a technical skill, but use the act of reading to name the world that had been organizing the silence. *Peasant. Landlord. Wage. Debt.* The words that reorganize the visible. The reading of the word as the reading of the world.

And this is what Gramsci did from his prison cell — built a map of the structures that felt like nature, named the mechanism of consent, traced the seal of hegemony so that anyone who read the notebooks could see it operating in their own experience.

---

One more name. My Sensei had it. My yoga teachers had it. My wilderness mentors had it — the ones I chose, not the ones assigned to me. They were unvarying. I spent years on the other names in this chapter, building the theory, accumulating the mistakes, convinced I was working toward something new. Connection was there at the beginning, in the room with the people who were trying to teach me, waiting for me to come back.

*Connection comes first.* Every time. Not knowledge. Not discipline. Not correctness. Not excellence. Connection.

If it is there, honor it. If it is not there, nothing can fake it.

I learned this the way you learn things at the restaurant — not from a book, not from a program, but from the specific exhaustion of having tried everything else first. The scaffold was real. The motivation was real. The care was real. But care that is not yet in contact with the other person is care talking to itself.

The commons is built from connection, not from correct design. The design matters — Ostrom's eight principles are real, and they work — but the design is downstream of something prior. The first thing that has to exist between people attempting to govern something together is: *I see you. I am here. We are in this together.* Before the rules. Before the governance structure. Before the plan.

This is the thing the enclosure cannot produce. It can produce compliance. It can produce performance. It can produce the appearance of connection, maintained by incentive. But the actual thing — the felt sense of being recognized, of mattering to the people you are with, of existing in their attention — this the enclosure systematically starves, because it is the condition for everything it cannot control.

The commons begins with: *I see you.* The rest is structure in service of that seeing.

If you are about to try to build something free — and I hope you are, because you are still reading — let this be the first check. Not: have I designed this correctly? Not: do I have the resources? Not: will it scale?

*Have I connected, first?*

The names in the spellbook are for what you are up against. This last one is for what you are building with.

*Solidarity.* The political name. The one working people have always used. The one act the demons have never been able to administer.

Solidarity is why Rosa Luxemburg starts the next chapter smiling.
